notes: Hamlet (2709) vs very weak opponents (1954-2139). Blue (Chess_Taifun 1954) gets 3 points — worst score in dataset. Blue checkmated round 14. All three weak players below 2150. Hamlet shows efficient domination: king walk (deliberate repositioning, not blunder), pawn storm + promotion, clinical mating attacks.

Red.notes: STRONG BUT UNORTHODOX (Hamlet 2709): Higher pawn ratio than usual (40%) — deliberate pawn storm strategy (f3, g4, k4, j3 prepare g-file attack). King manually repositioned (Kh1→i1→j1) instead of castling — intentional, not blunder. Delivers checkmate round 14. Then promotes g-pawn for a second queen. Adapts style to exploit weak opponents.

Blue.notes: WEAKEST IN DATASET (1954): 54% pawn ratio. Queen NEVER activated — still on a8 when checkmated round 14. 7 pawn pushes in 13 moves. 5 of first 6 moves are pawn pushes (b7-c7, b4-d4, b9-d9, b11-c11, b8-c8). Castles round 11 but queen stays dormant. Only 1 capture (recapture b6xNc5). Gets 3 points — worst result in entire dataset. This is what sub-2000 Elo play looks like.

Yellow.notes: WEAK-BUT-CASTLES-EARLY (2139): Castles round 7 (earliest in this game). But 45% pawn ratio — 3 pawn pushes in first 3 rounds. Queen late (round 11). Pushes h-pawn 4 times (h13→h11→h10→h9→h8→h7=D) — promotes but at cost of 5 tempo on one pawn. Captures material but passively. 30 points.

Green.notes: WEAK-BUT-ACTIVE-BISHOPS (2069): 40% pawn ratio. Both bishops developed and aggressively capture (Bl8xg13, Bg13xNf12, Bm10xBi14 — 3 bishop captures). Knight bounces (Nl4→n5 — retreats). Late castle (round 16). Queen active round 6 but mostly repositions. 30 points — ties Yellow.

Blue_1954: 54% pawn ratio — highest in entire dataset
5 of first 6 moves are pawn pushes
Queen NEVER activated (still on a8 at checkmate)
Checkmated round 14 with 3 points — worst result in dataset
This is what sub-2000 play looks like
Yellow_2139: h-pawn pushed 5 times (h13→h11→h10→h9→h8→h7=D) — 5 tempo for 1 promotion
Green_2069: Knight bounces: Nl4→n5 (develops then retreats to near-starting square)
40% pawn ratio with front-loaded pawn pushes

strong_player_adaptation.Hamlet_2709: Hamlet adapts strategy to opponent strength. Against very weak players, uses pawn storm + king walk (not castling) to deliver fast checkmate (round 14). Higher pawn ratio than normal (40% vs usual 20-30%) because the pawn storm IS the attack plan. Key insight: strong players adjust their style. The engine should have one consistent style that works at all levels, not try to emulate Hamlet's adaptive play.
